steps.title: Identify the sentence structure at the blank
steps.content: The sentence reads: “new types of polymers have been engineered to repair ______ aided by embedded microcapsules, such materials begin to self-heal.” After filling the blank, the portion before “aided” must be a complete independent clause (“...engineered to repair themselves”), and “Upon fracture, aided by embedded microcapsules, such materials begin to self-heal” is a second independent clause. Two independent clauses cannot be joined by a comma alone (comma splice) or left to run together.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the match
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'themselves, upon fracture,': ✗ Creates a comma splice — a comma alone cannot join two independent clauses.
  • Choice B — 'themselves—upon fracture—': ✗ The em dashes set off “upon fracture” as a parenthetical inside the first clause, but then “such materials begin to self-heal” is left as a run-on after the closing dash.
  • Choice C — 'themselves. Upon fracture,': ✓ A period ends the first independent clause cleanly; “Upon fracture, aided by embedded microcapsules, such materials begin to self-heal” then forms a correct new sentence.
  • Choice D — 'themselves upon fracture,': ✗ No punctuation between “themselves” and “upon fracture” runs the two clauses together without any boundary marker.
That matches choice C — C is correct.
